Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## SCORE-BASED GENERATIVE MODELING THROUGH STOCHASTIC DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
**Yang Song**[˚] **Jascha Sohl-Dickstein** Stanford University Google Brain yangsong@cs.stanford.edu jaschasd@google.com

**Diederik P. Kingma** Google Brain durk@google.com

**Abhishek Kumar Stefano Ermon Ben Poole** Google Brain Stanford University Google Brain abhishk@google.com ermon@cs.stanford.edu pooleb@google.com

## ABSTRACT

Creating noise from data is easy; creating data from noise is generative modeling. We present a stochastic differential equation (SDE) that smoothly transforms a complex data distribution to a known prior distribution by slowly injecting noise, and a corresponding reverse-time SDE that transforms the prior distribution back into the data distribution by slowly removing the noise. Crucially, the reverse-time SDE depends only on the time-dependent gradient field (a.k.a., score) of the perturbed data distribution. By leveraging advances in score-based generative modeling, we can accurately estimate these scores with neural networks, and use numerical SDE solvers to generate samples. We show that this framework encapsulates previous approaches in score-based generative modeling and diffusion probabilistic modeling, allowing for new sampling procedures and new modeling capabilities. In particular, we introduce a predictor-corrector framework to correct errors in the evolution of the discretized reverse-time SDE. We also derive an equivalent neural ODE that samples from the same distribution as the SDE, but additionally enables exact likelihood computation, and improved sampling efficiency. In addition, we provide a new way to solve inverse problems with score-based models, as demonstrated with experiments on class-conditional generation, image inpainting, and colorization. Combined with multiple architectural improvements, we achieve record-breaking performance for unconditional image generation on CIFAR-10 with an Inception score of 9.89 and FID of 2.20, a competitive likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim, and demonstrate high fidelity generation of 1024 ˆ 1024 images for the first time from a score-based generative model.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Two successful classes of probabilistic generative models involve sequentially corrupting training data with slowly increasing noise, and then learning to reverse this corruption in order to form a generative model of the data. _Score matching with Langevin dynamics_ (SMLD) (Song & Ermon, 2019) estimates the _score_ ( _i.e_ ., the gradient of the log probability density with respect to data) at each noise scale, and then uses Langevin dynamics to sample from a sequence of decreasing noise scales during generation. _Denoising diffusion probabilistic modeling_ (DDPM) (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015; Ho et al., 2020) trains a sequence of probabilistic models to reverse each step of the noise corruption, using knowledge of the functional form of the reverse distributions to make training tractable. For continuous state spaces, the DDPM training objective implicitly computes scores at each noise scale. We therefore refer to these two model classes together as _score-based generative models_ .

Score-based generative models, and related techniques (Bordes et al., 2017; Goyal et al., 2017; Du & Mordatch, 2019), have proven effective at generation of images (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020; Ho et al., 2020), audio (Chen et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2020), graphs (Niu et al., 2020), and shapes (Cai

et al., 2020). To enable new sampling methods and further extend the capabilities of score-based generative models, we propose a unified framework that generalizes previous approaches through the lens of stochastic differential equations (SDEs).

Specifically, instead of perturbing data with a finite number of noise distributions, we consider a continuum of distributions that evolve over time according to a diffusion process. This process progressively diffuses a data point into random noise, and is given by a prescribed SDE that does not depend on the data and has no trainable parameters. By reversing this process, we can smoothly mold random noise into data for sample generation. Crucially, this reverse process satisfies a reverse-time SDE (Anderson, 1982), which can be derived from the forward SDE given the score of the marginal probability densities as a function of time. We can therefore approximate the reverse-time SDE by training a time-dependent neural network to estimate the scores, and then produce samples using numerical SDE solvers. Our key idea is summarized in Fig. 1.

Our proposed framework has several theoretical and practical contributions:

**Flexible sampling and likelihood computation:** We can employ any general-purpose SDE solver to integrate the reverse-time SDE for sampling. In addition, we propose two special methods not viable for general SDEs: (i) Predictor-Corrector (PC) samplers that combine numerical SDE solvers with score-based MCMC approaches, such as Langevin MCMC (Parisi, 1981) and HMC (Neal et al., 2011); and (ii) deterministic samplers based on the probability flow ordinary differential equation (ODE). The former _unifies and improves_ over existing sampling methods for score-based models. The latter allows for _fast adaptive sampling_ via black-box ODE solvers, _flexible data manipulation_ via latent codes, a _uniquely identifiable encoding_ , and notably, _exact likelihood computation_ .

**Controllable generation:** We can modulate the generation process by conditioning on information not available during training, because the conditional reverse-time SDE can be efficiently estimated from _unconditional_ scores. This enables applications such as class-conditional generation, image inpainting, colorization and other inverse problems, all achievable using a single unconditional score-based model without re-training.

**Unified framework:** Our framework provides a unified way to explore and tune various SDEs for improving score-based generative models. The methods of SMLD and DDPM can be amalgamated into our framework as discretizations of two separate SDEs. Although DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) was recently reported to achieve higher sample quality than SMLD (Song & Ermon, 2019; 2020), we show that with better architectures and new sampling algorithms allowed by our framework, the latter can catch up—it achieves new state-of-the-art Inception score (9.89) and FID score (2.20) on CIFAR-10, as well as high-fidelity generation of 1024 ˆ 1024 images for the first time from a score-based model. In addition, we propose a new SDE under our framework that achieves a likelihood value of 2.99 bits/dim on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10 images, setting a new record on this task.

## 3 SCORE-BASED GENERATIVE MODELING WITH SDES

Perturbing data with multiple noise scales is key to the success of previous methods. We propose to generalize this idea further to an infinite number of noise scales, such that perturbed data distributions evolve according to an SDE as the noise intensifies. An overview of our framework is given in Fig. 2.

## 4.1 GENERAL-PURPOSE NUMERICAL SDE SOLVERS

Numerical solvers provide approximate trajectories from SDEs. Many general-purpose numerical methods exist for solving SDEs, such as Euler-Maruyama and stochastic Runge-Kutta methods (Kloeden & Platen, 2013), which correspond to different discretizations of the stochastic dynamics. We can apply any of them to the reverse-time SDE for sample generation.

Ancestral sampling, the sampling method of DDPM (Eq. (4)), actually corresponds to one special discretization of the reverse-time VP SDE (Eq. (11)) (see Appendix E). Deriving the ancestral sampling rules for new SDEs, however, can be non-trivial. To remedy this, we propose _reverse diffusion samplers_ (details in Appendix E), which discretize the reverse-time SDE in the same way as the forward one, and thus can be readily derived given the forward discretization. As shown in Table 1, reverse diffusion samplers perform slightly better than ancestral sampling for both SMLD and DDPM models on CIFAR-10 (DDPM-type ancestral sampling is also applicable to SMLD models, see Appendix F.)

## 4.2 PREDICTOR-CORRECTOR SAMPLERS

Unlike generic SDEs, we have additional information that can be used to improve solutions. Since we have a score-based model **s** _**θ**_ ˚p **x** _, t_ q « _∇_ **x** log _pt_ p **x** q, we can employ score-based MCMC approaches, such as Langevin MCMC (Parisi, 1981; Grenander & Miller, 1994) or HMC (Neal et al., 2011) to sample from _pt_ directly, and correct the solution of a numerical SDE solver.

Specifically, at each time step, the numerical SDE solver first gives an estimate of the sample at the next time step, playing the role of a “predictor”. Then, the score-based MCMC approach corrects the marginal distribution of the estimated sample, playing the role of a “corrector”. The idea is analogous to Predictor-Corrector methods, a family of numerical continuation techniques for solving systems of equations (Allgower & Georg, 2012), and we similarly name our hybrid sampling algorithms _Predictor-Corrector_ (PC) samplers. Please find pseudo-code and a complete description in Appendix G. PC samplers generalize the original sampling methods of SMLD and DDPM: the former uses an identity function as the predictor and annealed Langevin dynamics as the corrector, while the latter uses ancestral sampling as the predictor and identity as the corrector.

We test PC samplers on SMLD and DDPM models (see Algorithms 2 and 3 in Appendix G) trained with original discrete objectives given by Eqs. (1) and (3). This exhibits the compatibility of PC samplers to score-based models trained with a fixed number of noise scales. We summarize the performance of different samplers in Table 1, where probability flow is a predictor to be discussed in Section 4.3. Detailed experimental settings and additional results are given in Appendix G. We observe that our reverse diffusion sampler always outperform ancestral sampling, and corrector-only methods (C2000) perform worse than other competitors (P2000, PC1000) with the same computation (In fact, we need way more corrector steps per noise scale, and thus more computation, to match the performance of other samplers.) For all predictors, adding one corrector step for each predictor step (PC1000) doubles computation but always improves sample quality (against P1000). Moreover, it is typically better than doubling the number of predictor steps without adding a corrector (P2000), where we have to interpolate between noise scales in an ad hoc manner (detailed in Appendix G) for SMLD/DDPM models. ## 4.3 PROBABILITY FLOW AND CONNECTION TO NEURAL ODES

**Exact likelihood computation** Leveraging the connection to neural ODEs, we can compute the density defined by Eq. (13) via the instantaneous change of variables formula (Chen et al., 2018). This allows us to compute the _exact likelihood on any input data_ (details in Appendix D.2). Decoding can be achieved by integrating a corresponding ODE for the reverse-time SDE. As is done with other invertible models such as neural ODEs and normalizing flows (Dinh et al., 2016; Kingma & Dhariwal, 2018), we can manipulate this latent representation for image editing, such as interpolation, and temperature scaling (see Fig. 3 and Appendix D.4).

## 4.4 ARCHITECTURE IMPROVEMENTS

We explore several new architecture designs for score-based models using both VE and VP SDEs (details in Appendix H), where we train models with the same discrete objectives as in SMLD/DDPM. We directly transfer the architectures for VP SDEs to sub-VP SDEs due to their similarity. Our optimal architecture for the VE SDE, named NCSN++, achieves an FID of 2.45 on CIFAR-10 with PC samplers, while our optimal architecture for the VP SDE, called DDPM++, achieves 2.78.

By switching to the continuous training objective in Eq. (7), and increasing the network depth, we can further improve sample quality for all models. The resulting architectures are denoted as NCSN++ cont. and DDPM++ cont. in Table 3 for VE and VP/sub-VP SDEs respectively. Results reported in Table 3 are for the checkpoint with the smallest FID over the course of training, where samples are generated with PC samplers. In contrast, FID scores and NLL values in Table 2 are reported for the last training checkpoint, and samples are obtained with black-box ODE solvers. As shown in Table 3, VE SDEs typically provide better sample quality than VP/sub-VP SDEs, but we also empirically observe that their likelihoods are worse than VP/sub-VP SDE counterparts. This indicates that practitioners likely need to experiment with different SDEs for varying domains and architectures.

Our best model for sample quality, NCSN++ cont. (deep, VE), doubles the network depth and sets new records for both inception score and FID on unconditional generation for CIFAR-10. Surprisingly, we can achieve better FID than the previous best conditional generative model without requiring labeled data. With all improvements together, we also obtain the first set of high-fidelity samples on CelebA-HQ 1024 ˆ 1024 from score-based models (see Appendix H.3). Our best model for likelihoods, DDPM++ cont. (deep, sub-VP), similarly doubles the network depth and achieves a log-likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim with the continuous objective in Eq. (7). To our best knowledge, this is the highest likelihood on uniformly dequantized CIFAR-10.

Imputation amounts to sampling from _p_ p **x** p0q | Ωp **y** qq, which we can accomplish using an unconditional model (see Appendix I.2). Colorization is a special case of imputation, except that the known data dimensions are coupled. We can decouple these data dimensions with an orthogonal linear transformation, and perform imputation in the transformed space (details in Appendix I.3). Fig. 4 (right) shows results for inpainting and colorization achieved with unconditional time-dependent score-based models.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We presented a framework for score-based generative modeling based on SDEs. Our work enables a better understanding of existing approaches, new sampling algorithms, exact likelihood computation, uniquely identifiable encoding, latent code manipulation, and brings new conditional generation abilities to the family of score-based generative models.

While our proposed sampling approaches improve results and enable more efficient sampling, they remain slower at sampling than GANs (Goodfellow et al., 2014) on the same datasets. Identifying ways of combining the stable learning of score-based generative models with the fast sampling of implicit models like GANs remains an important research direction. Additionally, the breadth of samplers one can use when given access to score functions introduces a number of hyper-parameters. Future work would benefit from improved methods to automatically select and tune these hyperparameters, as well as more extensive investigation on the merits and limitations of various samplers.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper represents a landmark contribution to generative modeling, offering a rigorous and elegant unification of score-matching and diffusion-based methods through the framework of Stochastic Differential Equations (SDEs). The clarity of the writing is exceptional, successfully bridging the gap between discrete-time transitions and continuous-time diffusion processes. Its quality is evidenced by both theoretical depth—the derivation of the reverse-time SDE and the "probability flow" ODE—and impressive empirical results, including state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-10 and high-resolution image synthesis. The introduction of Predictor-Corrector samplers and exact likelihood computation via neural ODEs addresses several previous limitations of score-based models simultaneously. The most significant potential issue for a reader is the high mathematical barrier to entry; the paper assumes a firm grasp of stochastic calculus and Anderson’s theorem, which may mask some of the practical complexities involved in choosing the specific drift and diffusion coefficients ($f$ and $g$) for new domains. Additionally, while the framework enables "controllable generation" without retraining, the computational cost of the SDE solvers compared to traditional GANs remains a vital practical consideration that warrants further discussion. Overall, this is a foundational work that fundamentally shifts the landscape of probabilistic modeling.

comments.title: Missing coefficient in sub-VP SDE definition
comments.quote: $$d\mathbf{x} = -\frac{1}{2}\beta(t)\mathbf{x} dt + \sqrt{\beta(t)(1 - e^{-2\int_0^t \beta(s)ds})} d\mathbf{w}$$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.4, Equation (12) defines the 'sub-VP SDE'. The authors claim this SDE's variance is always bounded by the VP SDE. For the VP SDE (Eq. 11), the diffusion coefficient is $g(t) = \sqrt{\beta(t)}$. In Eq. (12), the diffusion coefficient is significantly more complex: $g(t) = \sqrt{\beta(t)(1 - e^{-2\int_0^t \beta(s)ds})}$. While this is a novel SDE proposed by the authors, throughout the summary and later sections, $g(t)$ is generally treated as a scalar independent of $x$. This formula is correct for the intended 'sub-VP' behavior, but it introduces a very complex time-dependency in $g(t)$ that makes the 'standard techniques' for closed-form Gaussian transition kernels mentioned in Section 3.3 much more mathematically involved than the VP case. There is no technical error in the formula itself, but there is a potential for reader confusion regarding the tractability of the transition kernel $p_{0t}$ for this specific SDE compared to VE/VP, which is not fully addressed until the appendices.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Probability Flow' predictor comparison
comments.quote: In Table 1, 'probabilityfow' is listed as a 'Predictor' for the PC sampler.
comments.explanation: The table header defines 'PC1000' as using 1000 predictor and 1000 corrector steps. When 'probability flow' (the deterministic ODE mentioned in Section 4.3) is used as the 'Predictor', it is not immediately clear how a single step of an ODE solver integrates into the PC framework which usually assumes a stochastic predictor step. Specifically, if the ODE is deterministic, applying a Langevin 'corrector' (which is stochastic) effectively turns the deterministic path back into a stochastic one. The text in Section 4.2 says 'PC samplers generalize... SMLD and DDPM', but the use of the Probability Flow ODE as a predictor in a PC cycle is a distinct hybrid that is under-defined in the main text. A reader might struggle to reproduce the 'PC1000' results for the 'probabilityfow' row without knowing if the corrector step uses the same noise schedule as the SDE or something derived from the ODE's marginals.

comments.title: Possible contradiction in predictor-corrector naming convention
comments.quote: PC samplers generalize the original sampling methods of SMLD and DDPM: the former uses an identity function as the predictor and annealed Langevin dynamics as the corrector, while the latter uses ancestral sampling as the predictor and identity as the corrector.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.1, SMLD is described as running $M$ steps of Langevin MCMC for each noise scale $\sigma_i$. In the PC framework of Section 4.2, the authors define a 'predictor' as an SDE solver (like Euler-Maruyama) that moves between noise scales, and a 'corrector' as an MCMC step at a fixed noise scale. The text claims SMLD uses an 'identity predictor.' However, standard SMLD (Eq. 2) initializes $\mathbf{x}_i^0 = \mathbf{x}_{i+1}^M$. While this is an identity mapping of the state, SMLD as originally proposed inherently moves through noise scales. Similarly, for DDPM, ancestral sampling (Eq. 4) is a single-step transition that changes the noise level. Labeling these as 'identity' predictors/correctors is a helpful conceptual mapping for their new framework, but it might confuse a reader looking for a traditional 'predictor' step in the 2015/2020 papers. However, since the authors explicitly provide the mapping logic in the text, it is a matter of nomenclature rather than a technical error.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in 'exact' likelihood vs. dequantization
comments.quote: This allows us to compute the exact likelihood on any input data... We compute log-likelihoods on uniformly dequantized data, and only compare to models evaluated in the same way
comments.explanation: The authors claim 'exact likelihood' computation via the Probability Flow ODE. In the context of generative modeling for discrete data (images), 'exact' usually contrasts with 'variational lower bounds' (ELBO). However, because they use 'uniform dequantization' (adding noise to discrete pixels to make them continuous), the likelihood being computed is for the continuous proxy distribution, not the discrete density of the original pixels. While this is standard practice in the flow-based modeling literature (like Glow or RealNVP cited in Table 2), a reader might confuse 'exact likelihood' with 'likelihood of the discrete data.' The qualifier about dequantization is present, but the 'exact' claim is highlighted as a primary contribution. This is a minor framing issue common in the field, rather than a mathematical error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the PC1000 sampler's computational cost
comments.quote: adding one corrector step for each predictor step (PC1000) doubles computation but always improves sample quality (against P1000). Moreover, it is typically better than doubling the number of predictor steps without adding a corrector (P2000)
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the 'NFE' (Number of Function Evaluations) is the standard metric for cost. The text implies PC1000 (1000 predictor steps + 1000 corrector steps) has the same cost as P2000 (2000 predictor steps). This assumes that one 'step' of a corrector (Langevin MCMC) and one 'step' of a predictor (SDE discretization) each require exactly one evaluation of the score function $\mathbf{s}_\theta$. While this is generally true for the algorithms described (Langevin requires the gradient once, and Euler-Maruyama requires the drift/score once), some higher-order SDE predictors or MCMC schemes might require multiple evaluations. The text doesn't explicitly state '1 step = 1 NFE' until the caption of Table 1 mentions 'shaded regions are obtained with the same computation.' This is a minor point of clarity for reproducibility.

comments.title: Vague justification for the conditional reverse SDE formulation
comments.quote: $d \mathbf{x} = [\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}, t) - g(t)^2 [ \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{y} | \mathbf{x})]]dt + g(t)d \bar{\mathbf{w}}.$
comments.explanation:
In Section 5, the authors introduce the conditional reverse-time SDE by adding the gradient of the log-likelihood $
abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{y} | \mathbf{x})$ to the unconditional score. While this is a standard application of Bayes' rule in score-based modeling ($
abla \log p(x|y) = 
abla \log p(x) + 
abla \log p(y|x)$), the transition from Eq. (6) to Eq. (14) assumes that the conditional distribution $p_t(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{y})$ evolves according to a reverse-time SDE with the same diffusion coefficient $g(t)$ and a drift modified by the conditional score. While this is mathematically sound under the Anderson (1982) framework, the paper suggests that image inpainting and colorization (inverse problems) can be solved using Eq. (14) with an *unconditional* model. However, for inpainting, $
abla_{\mathbf{x}} \log p_t(\mathbf{y} | \mathbf{x})$ is often a Dirac delta or a hard constraint, making the gradient undefined or infinite. The text mentions 'heuristics and domain knowledge' and 'broadly applicable methods' in the Appendix, but the main text's claim that Eq. (14) 'enables applications such as image inpainting' is technically underspecified because Eq. (14) requires a differentiable guidance term, which standard inpainting constraints do not provide without specific approximations (like projection or Replacement/Manifold sampling).

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'uniquely identifiable encoding' claim
comments.quote: The latter allows for ... a uniquely identifiable encoding
comments.explanation: The abstract and conclusion claim the Probability Flow ODE enables a 'uniquely identifiable encoding.' In the context of generative modeling, 'identifiability' usually refers to the ability to recover true latent parameters or the uniqueness of the model parameters. Given the context of Section 4.3 (Probability Flow ODE), the authors likely mean that the mapping between the data distribution and the prior is bijective and deterministic, allowing a data point to be mapped to a unique latent code. However, 'identifiable' is a loaded term in statistics. While the path is unique for a fixed score function, the claim might be misinterpreted as a statement about the statistical identifiability of the model's parameters or the latent space structure, which is not proved here. The justification provided in the summary ('forward SDE has no trainable parameters') supports the uniqueness of the mapping, but the phrasing remains slightly ambiguous.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a significant and technically sophisticated advancement in the field of generative modeling by unifying and extending score-based and diffusion-based approaches through the principled lens of stochastic differential equations (SDEs). The work is well-written, logically structured, and clearly articulates a powerful theoretical framework that not only subsumes prior methods—such as denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPM) and score matching with Langevin dynamics (SMLD)—but also enables a host of new capabilities, including more flexible sampling schemes, exact likelihood computation via an equivalent ODE formulation, and improved performance on standard benchmarks. The core insight—reversing a forward SDE that diffuses data into noise by estimating time-dependent score functions—is theoretically sound and elegantly developed. The introduction of a predictor-corrector sampling framework and the ability to solve inverse problems using a single unconditional model are particularly compelling contributions. Empirically, the results are state-of-the-art, with exceptional performance on CIFAR-10 and groundbreaking generation of high-resolution images, underscoring the practical impact of the proposed framework. That said, one notable issue lies in the clarity of presentation for readers less familiar with SDEs and score-based modeling—the exposition, particularly in the background and early technical sections, assumes substantial prior knowledge, and could benefit from more intuitive explanations or visualizations to bridge the gap between theory and practice. Additionally, while the paper claims broad applicability, some methodological details—such as network architecture choices or the specific form of the proposed SDEs—are mentioned only briefly and may limit reproducibility. Overall, however, this is a high-quality, impactful paper that significantly advances the state of the art in generative modeling with a rigorous and unifying theoretical perspective.

comments.title: Inconsistent treatment of weighting function λ(t) in continuous score matching objective
comments.quote: As in SMLD and DDPM, we can typically choose λ ∝1{E“  ∇_ **x** p _t_ q log _p_ 0 _t_ p **x** p _t_ q | **x** p0qq2 ‰.
comments.explanation: The expression involves an expectation over the conditional score: E[‖∇ₓ log p₀ₜ(x(t)|x(0))‖²], and suggests setting λ(t) ∝ 1 / E[‖∇ₓ log p(x(t)|x(0))‖²], implying that higher-variance score directions are down-weighted. However, in prior work (e.g., Song et al., 2019a; Hyvärinen, 2005), the optimal weighting for variance reduction in denoising score matching is proportional to the variance of the noise perturbation, which for Gaussian processes leads to λ(σ) ∝ σ², or in continuous time, λ(t) ∝ g(t)². For many SDEs (e.g., VP), the score norm increases over time, so 1/E[∇ log p]^2 would decrease over time—potentially counteracting the desired stabilization. The correct justification relies on g(t)² scaling, as seen in practice (e.g., in DDPM). While the paper presents this as a heuristic choice, attributing it to inverse score norm misleads readers about the theoretical foundation. The actual training likely uses standard practice (confirmed in appendix), but the stated rationale is reversed. This is a moderate issue because it misattributes a key design choice.

comments.title: Unclear justification for equivalence between ancestral sampling and reverse-time SDE discretization
comments.quote: Ancestral sampling, the sampling method of DDPM (Eq. (4)), actually corresponds to one special discretization of the reverse-time VP SDE (Eq. (11)) (see Appendix E).
comments.explanation: This claim is central to the paper's unification narrative: that DDPM's ancestral sampling emerges as a discretization of the reverse-time SDE. While this is true under proper reparameterization (e.g., Ho et al. show how predicted noise connects to score estimation), the main text provides no derivation or even a sketch of this connection. The reverse-time SDE has drift f(x,t) − g(t)²∇ₓ log pₜ(x), and ancestral sampling uses a learned mean that implicitly encodes the score via model parameterization. For the equivalence to hold, one must show equivalence between the network output and the score under the training objective. Without this, the claim appears asserted rather than derived. The paper references Appendix E, but omitting even a high-level explanation in the main text weakens transparency. Given that this underpins the unified framework, the lack of accessible justification is a moderate shortcoming.

comments.title: VE/VP SDE limit derivation assumes N→∞ without addressing convergence of discrete chains
comments.quote: In the limit of _N_ Ñ 8, t _σi_ u _[N] i_ “1 becomes a function _σ_ [p _t_ q], ... and the Markov chain t **x** _i_ u _[N] i_ “1 becomes a continuous stochastic process t **x** p _t_ qu[1] _t_ “0
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that as N→∞, the discrete Markov chain of SMLD/DDPM models converges to a continuous SDE (VE or VP), which is crucial for the unification framework. While the conclusion is correct and aligns with known results, the argument lacks formal grounding. No reference is made to convergence theorems for stochastic processes (e.g., Kurtz’s theorem or diffusion limits of Markov chains), nor are scaling assumptions (e.g., Δt = 1/N) explicitly discussed. Although the derived SDEs match standard forms, omitting theoretical justification for this limit leaves a foundational gap. Experts may accept the result, but a rigorous treatment would strengthen the paper. This constitutes a moderate issue: not an error, but a missing theoretical pillar for a core claim.

comments.title: OCR-related rendering issues in key equations
comments.explanation: Multiple equations suffer from OCR artifacts that affect readability but not substance. These include: (1) missing tildes (e.g., 'pσ(x|x)' instead of 'pσ(x|\tilde{x})') in perturbation kernels, creating confusion between noisy and clean data; (2) '[?]' replacing the square root symbol in the DDPM transition kernel, where the mean should be √(1−β_i) x_{i−1}; and (3) '[2]' incorrectly rendered as a subscript instead of superscript for g(t)^2 in the reverse SDE. In all cases, context and standard SMLD/DDPM formulations confirm the intended meaning (e.g., marginal variances rely on √α_i, reverse SDE includes g(t)^2). While no actual errors are introduced—since the authors clearly understand the correct forms—the rendering issues could mislead readers unfamiliar with the conventions. These should be corrected in the final version for clarity, especially as they cluster in critical sections. OCR warnings were noted, so these are not author errors, but collectively they warrant attention.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation and inconsistent indexing in SDE and diffusion modeling framework
comments.quote: Crucially, this reverse process satisfies a reverse-time SDE (Anderson, 1982), which can be derived from the forward SDE given the score of the marginal probability densities as a function of time.
comments.explanation: Several minor notational ambiguities arise from underspecification and inconsistent indexing: (1) The phrase 'score of the marginal probability densities' initially lacks clear time indexing; though clarified later as ∇ₓ log pₜ(x), early text does not define pₜ(x) := marginal density of x(t), risking confusion with conditionals; (2) The reverse-time SDE description mentions d\bar{w} and negative timesteps but omits explicit diffusion term g(t)^2 in the qualitative description, potentially misleading readers about its form; (3) Langevin dynamics use x_i^0 = x_{i+1}^M which, while standard, reuses x_i^0 for each iteration and could be misinterpreted as a fixed initialization. These are resolved contextually or in later equations, and no actual errors occur. However, consolidating these reveals a pattern of underspecified notation in central definitions, which could hinder reproducibility for less experienced readers. These are minor issues in isolation, but together suggest a need for more precise initial definitions.

comments.title: Overstatements and framing issues in claims about likelihood, sampling, and generalization
comments.quote: notably, _exact likelihood computation_
comments.explanation: The paper makes several strong claims that are technically correct in theory but practically overstated: (1) 'Exact likelihood computation' via the probability flow ODE is highlighted as a key benefit, but this requires perfect integration and trace estimation (e.g., Hutchinson’s trick), which are intractable in practice—thus, reported values (e.g., 2.99 bits/dim) are approximations. This should be qualified; (2) The claim that 'reverse diffusion sampler always outperforms ancestral sampling' generalizes from narrow experiments (CIFAR-10, specific models); 'always' overreaches without qualification; (3) Comparisons between PC1000 and P2000 equate 'computation' with step counts, ignoring that corrector steps may be more expensive per iteration; (4) Table 2 and Table 3 use different checkpoints (final vs. best-FID), conflating evaluation metrics across tables. While experts may recognize these nuances, the framing risks misleading readers. These are minor issues individually, but collectively they represent a tendency toward aggressive presentation that could affect interpretation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in PC sampler description and table presentation
comments.quote: We summarize the performance of different samplers in Table 1, where probability flow is a predictor to be discussed in Section 4.3.
comments.explanation: The text mislabels 'probability flow' as a 'predictor' in the PC framework, but in context it refers to a deterministic ODE-based sampler, not a predictor step in an SDE solver. Table 1 lists 'probability flow' alongside 'P1000', 'C2000', etc., suggesting it is a direct alternative, yet the description incorrectly frames it as a component of PC. This creates confusion about whether ODE solvers are part of the predictor-corrector framework or standalone methods. Similarly, the comparison between C2000 and PC1000 is muddled: the text claims 'same computation' but then admits correctors require more steps to match performance, obscuring their intended role as corrective rather than standalone. These presentation choices blur conceptual distinctions between sampling paradigms (SDE vs ODE, predictor vs sampler). While clarified later, the main text should avoid such ambiguity. This is a minor issue of clarity in exposition.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for score function in reverse-time SDE derivation
comments.quote: Crucially, this reverse process satisfies a reverse-time SDE (Anderson, 1982), which can be derived from the forward SDE given the score of the marginal probability densities as a function of time.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the reference to 'the score of the marginal probability densities' seems underspecified—does it mean ∇ₓ log pₜ(x) for each t? Later in Section 3.2, the reverse-time SDE is correctly introduced with the score ∇ₓ log pₜ(x), so the intended meaning is clarified. However, in Section 3.1, when defining pₜ(x), no subscript convention is explicitly established for time dependence beyond stating that pₜ(x) is the density of x(t). Given that rigorous stochastic processes require care in indexing distributions by time, and that confusion could arise between conditional and marginal densities (e.g., p(x(t)|x(0)) vs. pₜ(x)), the initial phrasing risks ambiguity. A more precise early statement—such as defining pₜ(x) := marginal density of x(t)—would help. Still, the rest of the paper uses ∇ₓ log pₜ(x) consistently, so the issue is resolved contextually. This is a minor framing concern rather than an actual error.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in perturbation kernel notation affecting readability
comments.explanation: The symbol **x** appears both as the observed variable and as the latent clean data, but due to OCR limitations, the tilde over the second **x** is rendered inconsistently. The text reads: '_pσ_ p **x** | **x** q', but based on context and standard notation in denoising score matching, this should be '_pσ_ p **x** | **x̃** q' where **x̃** denotes the clean data. In the original SMLD literature (Song & Ermon, 2019), the forward process is defined as corrupting a clean data point **x̃** ∼ p_data to obtain **x** via additive noise. The PDF uses a tilde in nearby expressions (e.g., 'p̃_σ(x | x̃)'), but here it appears garbled. While this is likely an OCR artifact (note: '˜ ˜ ˜ ˜' appears above the equation), the absence of clear distinction between **x** and **x̃** in the rendered text could mislead a reader into thinking both variables are the same. Context resolves this—later in the paragraph, integrals involve 'p_data(**x̃**) pσ(**x| x̃**)d **x̃**', confirming the intended meaning—but the current rendering leaves room for confusion. Since the intended meaning is recoverable from context and standard conventions, and since OCR issues are noted in the instructions, this is not an author error per se, but worth flagging if the final PDF has similar rendering problems.

comments.title: Inconsistent treatment of weighting function λ(t) in continuous score matching objective
comments.quote: As in SMLD and DDPM, we can typically choose λ ∝1{E“  ∇_ **x** p _t_ q log _p_ 0 _t_ p **x** p _t_ q | **x** p0qq2 ‰.
comments.explanation: The expression involves an expectation over the conditional score: E[‖∇ₓ log p₀ₜ(x(t)|x(0))‖²], and suggests setting λ(t) inversely proportional to this quantity. However, in prior work like Song et al. (2019a), the optimal weighting for denoising score matching is proportional to the variance of the score estimator, which for Gaussian perturbations leads to λ(σ) ∝ σ² or equivalent forms. In the continuous-time limit, this should correspond to λ(t) ∝ g(t)², where g(t) is the diffusion coefficient (as derived in e.g., Hyvärinen, 2005; Song et al., 2021). The current phrasing suggests λ(t) ∝ 1 / E[‖∇ₓ log p(x(t)|x(0))‖²], but for many SDEs (e.g., VP SDE), this expectation grows with time t, which would make λ(t) decrease—potentially counteracting the desired variance stabilization. The correct choice, as confirmed in later works (e.g., 'Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models' by Ho et al. implicitly use weights related to noise schedule), is λ(t) ∝ g(t)² or equivalent. While the paper says 'typically choose', implying heuristic usage, this justification appears reversed. However, in Appendix B and related derivations, the actual implementation likely follows standard practice. Thus, while the stated rationale may be misleading, the method itself is sound. This is a moderate issue because it misattributes justification, potentially leading readers to incorrect weighting choices if taken literally.

comments.title: Unclear justification for equivalence between ancestral sampling and reverse-time SDE discretization
comments.quote: Ancestral sampling, the sampling method of DDPM (Eq. (4)), actually corresponds to one special discretization of the reverse-time VP SDE (Eq. (11)) (see Appendix E).
comments.explanation: This is a significant conceptual claim: that DDPM’s ancestral sampling implements a numerical scheme for the reverse-time SDE. While this is true under specific conditions (e.g., with Euler-Maruyama discretization and appropriate reparameterization), the claim requires more justification in the main text. Namely, the reverse-time SDE includes a score term (∇ₓ log pₜ(x)), and ancestral sampling uses a parameterized mean function derived from a model predicting noise or clean data. For the equivalence to hold, one must show that the learned model corresponds to the true score under the model’s assumptions—which depends on the parameterization and training objective. The appendix may contain this derivation, but in the absence of even a sketch, a reader cannot verify whether this holds generally or only asymptotically (e.g., with infinite data and perfect training). This is non-trivial because the reverse-time SDE has a drift term f(x,t) − g(t)²∇ₓ log pₜ(x), and ancestral sampling implicitly approximates this via a network output. Without linking the network output to the score (via reparameterization like in Ho et al.), the equivalence is unjustified. Given that this underpins the unification claim, and the paper positions this as a core insight, the lack of derivation or citation to such a derivation is a moderate shortcoming.

comments.title: Overstatement of 'exact likelihood computation' capability without qualification
comments.quote: notably, _exact likelihood computation_
comments.explanation: The claim that the probability flow ODE enables 'exact likelihood computation' is standard in the literature and correctly refers to the fact that one can compute the exact log-likelihood via the change-of-variable formula along the deterministic trajectory (as in Chen et al., 2018; Grathwohl et al., 2019). However, this computation requires solving a numerically unstable ODE and evaluating the trace of the Jacobian ∇ₓ·f(x,t), which is approximated in practice using Hutchinson’s estimator or neural networks. The paper does not clarify that while the likelihood is 'exact' in theory (under perfect integration and score estimation), in practice it is intractable to compute exactly due to numerical errors and architectural approximations. This could mislead readers unfamiliar with normalizing flows into thinking that practical evaluations yield true exact log-likelihoods. Later sections (e.g., reporting 2.99 bits/dim) obviously rely on approximate methods, but the emphasis on 'exact' without qualification in the contribution list may overstate the practical utility. Given that experts in the field understand these limitations, the claim is justifiable in spirit, but mild overreach in presentation. This is a minor issue of framing.

comments.title: VE/VP SDE limit derivation assumes N→∞ without addressing convergence of discrete chains
comments.quote: In the limit of _N_ Ñ 8, t _σi_ u _[N] i_ “1 becomes a function _σ_ [p _t_ q], ... and the Markov chain t **x** _i_ u _[N] i_ “1 becomes a continuous stochastic process t **x** p _t_ qu[1] _t_ “0
comments.explanation: The argument that discrete noise scales {σ_i} induce an SDE in the limit N→∞ relies on intuitive reasoning rather than formal convergence. While correct in spirit (diffusion limits of Markov chains converge weakly to solutions of SDEs under certain regularity conditions), the paper does not cite or invoke a formal result (e.g., Kurtz’s theorem or functional central limit theorems). This is particularly relevant because the discrete update rules (e.g., Eq. (8)) must be properly scaled (e.g., step size Δt = 1/N) to ensure convergence. The authors do correctly identify the resulting SDEs (VE and VP), matching known continuous limits, so the conclusion is valid. Still, omitting any reference to convergence theory (even in an appendix) for such a foundational argument constitutes a gap in justification. Since the claim is central to the unification framework (linking SMLD/DDPM to SDEs), this constitutes a moderate issue: not an error in result, but a missing theoretical pillar that supports their key insight.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in DDPM transition kernel due to OCR error
comments.explanation: The expression '[?]' appears in the mean of the Gaussian for the DDPM forward process, which is clearly an OCR misread of the square root symbol. From the surrounding context and standard DDPM formulation (e.g., Ho et al. 2020), the correct mean should be √(1 - β_i) **x**_{i-1}, not (1 - β_i) **x**_{i-1}. The rest of the section correctly defines α_i as the product of (1 - β_j), implying α_i = ∏(1 - β_j) and thus the marginal distribution depends on √α_i. This suggests the intended expression is √(1 - β_i). The later equation _pαi_ p **x** _i_ | **x** 0q = _N_ p **x** _i_ ; √α_i **x** 0, (1 - α_i) **I** ) confirms that scaling by the square root is expected. Without the square root, the process would inject far more noise than standard DDPM, contradicting known results. Thus, the '[?]' is a clear OCR artifact, but the underlying equation would be a serious error if literally interpreted.

comments.title: Incorrect diffusion coefficient notation in reverse-time SDE
comments.quote: where **w** ¯ is a standard Wiener process when time flows backwards from _T_ to 0, and d _t_ is an infinitesimal negative timestep.
comments.explanation: The reverse-time SDE (Eq. 6, not shown in full but referenced) is expected to have a diffusion coefficient g(t)^2, as per Anderson (1982) and standard results in reverse-time diffusions. The authors define g(t) as the forward diffusion coefficient, but do not explicitly state the reverse SDE in full form here. However, in standard theory, the reverse-time SDE should be dx = [f(x, t) - g(t)^2 ∇_x log p_t(x)] dt + g(t) d\bar{w}, where d\bar{w} is backward Wiener process. The absence of a corrected diffusion term (like g(t)^2) in the description, combined with the fact that later methods (VE, VP) rely on correct reverse drift, suggests a potential ambiguity. However, given that the authors reference Anderson (1982) and later successfully derive consistent SDEs (VE, VP), it's likely the full equation is correct in the original. This passage only describes it qualitatively, so while the omission is concerning, it may be a simplification for presentation. Still, a reader might be misled about the form of the reverse diffusion.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'same functional form' for weights in Eq. (1) and Eq. (3)
comments.quote: Notably, the weights of the _i_ -th summand in Eq. (1) and Eq. (3), namely _σi_[2][and][ p][1][´] _[α][i]_[q][, are related to corresponding perturbation kernels in the] same functional form: _σi_[2][9][1][{][E][r] _[∥∇]_ **[x]**[ log] _[ p][σ] i_[p] **[x]**[˜][ |] **[ x]**[q] _[∥]_[2] 2[s][ and][ p][1][ ´] _[ α][i]_[q9][1][{][E][r] _[∥∇]_ **[x]**[ log] _[ p][α][i]_[p] **[x]**[˜][ |] **[ x]**[q] _[∥]_[2] 2[s][.
comments.explanation: The passage claims that the weights σ_i^2 and (1 - α_i) are related to the expected squared norm of the score of the conditional distribution in the 'same functional form'. However, the actual expressions shown (proportional to the inverse of the expected squared score norm) suggest that the weight should be chosen as λ_i ∝ 1 / E[||∇_x log p(\tilde{x}|x)||^2], which makes sense for variance reduction in score matching. The text is ambiguous about what "same functional form" means—does it mean both weights are proportional to the variance of the perturbation kernel? For SMLD, σ_i^2 is the variance, and for DDPM, (1 - α_i) is the variance of the marginal. So yes, both weights are equal to the marginal variance, which is indeed the same functional role. However, the surrounding equations suggest λ_i ∝ variance, which contradicts λ_i ∝ 1 / E[||score||^2] unless that expectation is inversely proportional to variance. In fact, for Gaussian perturbations, E[||∇_x log p(\tilde{x}|x)||^2] = d / σ^2, so 1 / E[...] ∝ σ^2, which *is* consistent. So the claim is correct, but the explanation is confusingly phrased. A reader might initially think there's a contradiction, but it resolves upon deeper inspection.

comments.title: Incorrect indexing in Langevin dynamics description
comments.explanation: The description says x_i^0 = x_{i+1}^M when i < N. But the indexing implies decreasing i from N down to 1, so when i = N, we start with x_N^0 ~ N(0, σ_max^2 I). Then for i = N-1, i-1 = N-2, etc. The update says x_i^0 = x_{i+1}^M, which would mean x_{N-1}^0 = x_N^M, correct. But when i=1, it would set x_1^0 = x_2^M, which is fine. However, the final sample is x_M^1, which is from the last chain. The issue is notational: using **x**_i^{[m]} where i indexes the noise scale and m the MCMC step, but then reusing **x**_i^0 to mean the initialization for scale i, set from the *final* sample of the *next* (noisier) scale. This is correct and standard in SMLD, but the subscript notation is ambiguous. In particular, x_i^0 is redefined at each step. This is not uncommon but could mislead a careful reader into thinking x_i^0 is a fixed initialization. However, the method is correctly described and matches Song & Ermon (2019), so while the notation is slightly unclear, it is not erroneous.

comments.title: Ambiguity in PC sampler computation comparison
comments.quote: For all predictors, adding one corrector step for each predictor step (PC1000) doubles computation but always improves sample quality (against P1000). Moreover, it is typically better than doubling the number of predictor steps without adding a corrector (P2000), where we have to interpolate between noise scales in an ad hoc manner (detailed in Appendix G) for SMLD/DDPM models.
comments.explanation: At first, the statement that 'PC1000 doubles computation' compared to P1000 seems clear: 1000 predictor and 1000 corrector steps imply 2000 total steps versus 1000. However, the comparison with P2000 (2000 predictor steps) raises a subtle issue. The text claims PC1000 is 'typically better' than P2000, implying a favorable trade-off: similar computation but better sample quality. Yet, the computation cost of PC1000 involves two different types of steps—SDE prediction and MCMC correction—each with potentially different computational loads. In contrast, P2000 uses only predictor steps. While Table 1 confirms this trend with FID values, the prose oversimplifies by equating 'number of steps' with 'computation,' ignoring potential differences in per-step cost (e.g., corrector steps may require additional gradient evaluations or MCMC iterations). The phrase 'interpolate between noise scales in an ad hoc manner' suggests that P2000 requires heuristic adjustments not needed in PC1000, but this is only 'detailed in Appendix G'—leaving the main text underspecified. A reader might incorrectly assume that computation is purely step-count, without considering algorithmic differences. This could mislead reproduction efforts.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'reverse diffusion sampler' vs. forward discretization
comments.quote: We observe that our reverse diffusion sampler always outperform ancestral sampling...
comments.explanation: The term 'reverse diffusion sampler' is introduced in Section 4.1 as a method that discretizes the reverse-time SDE in the same way as the forward SDE. This contrasts with ancestral sampling, which is a specific discretization of the reverse-time VP SDE. However, the claim that 'reverse diffusion sampler always outperform ancestral sampling' is based on Table 1, where both are applied to models based on VE and VP SDEs. For the VP SDE case (DDPM), ancestral sampling is the standard method; thus, stating it is outperformed by the reverse diffusion sampler implies a different discretization is being used. This is valid, as Appendix E justifies, but the main text doesn't clarify that the 'reverse diffusion sampler' uses a symmetric discretization (like forward Euler) while ancestral sampling uses a reweighted, variational approximation. Without seeing Appendix E, a reader might incorrectly infer that both methods are doing fundamentally similar things, when in fact they differ in underlying assumptions and implementation. The distinction is technically sound (and authors rightly note the reverse diffusion method is more general), but the comparison lacks nuance in the prose.

comments.title: Unjustified generalization from limited ablation in Table 1
comments.quote: We observe that our reverse diffusion sampler always outperform ancestral sampling...
comments.explanation: The word 'always' suggests a universal superiority of reverse diffusion samplers over ancestral sampling across all models and SDEs. However, the evidence in Table 1 is limited to CIFAR-10, VE and VP SDEs, and specific architectures (implied by model names). While the result may hold broadly, claiming 'always' overgeneralizes from a narrow empirical setting. In practice, ancestral sampling is derived from a variational principle and may outperform a crude reverse discretization under certain conditions (e.g., coarse time steps or low data dimensionality). The authors are correct that reverse diffusion provides a systematic generalization, but asserting consistent superiority without further evidence or qualification (e.g., 'in our experiments') risks overstating. This is a mild overclaim resolvable from context (readers can see it's based on one dataset), so it's minor.

comments.title: Potential confusion between ODE sampler and PC sampler in Table 1
comments.quote: We summarize the performance of different samplers in Table 1, where probability flow is a predictor to be discussed in Section 4.3.
comments.explanation: The text introduces 'probability flow' as a predictor in the PC framework, yet in Table 1, 'probability flow' appears as a sampler under the same column as ancestral sampling and reverse diffusion. This risks confusion: is 'probability flow' a predictor within a PC scheme, or a standalone sampler? From Section 4.3, it's clear that solving the probability flow ODE yields a deterministic sampler, which can be used independently or within a hybrid framework. However, the placement in Table 1 alongside 'P1000', 'C2000', and 'PC1000'—which are SDE-based methods with explicit step counts—suggests it's being compared as a direct alternative. The explanation that 'probability flow is a predictor' is misleading, because in the ODE context, it's not a predictor for an SDE solver but a completely different sampling path. The authors do describe it correctly in Section 4.3, but the labeling in Table 1 and the reference in the passage create ambiguity about the role of ODE-based sampling in the PC framework. A careful reader might wonder whether 'PC + ODE' is being proposed, but this isn't elaborated.

comments.title: Misleading comparison of corrector-only versus predictor-corrector in computation
comments.quote: corrector-only methods (C2000) perform worse than other competitors (P2000, PC1000) with the same computation (In fact, we need way more corrector steps per noise scale, and thus more computation, to match the performance of other samplers.)
comments.explanation: The parentheses create a contradiction. First, the sentence states that C2000 is evaluated 'with the same computation' as P2000 and PC1000. But then it clarifies that 'we need way more corrector steps... to match performance,' implying that C2000 does *not* actually have the same computation-to-performance ratio. This confuses the reader: is C2000 being compared at equal step count or equal performance? Table 1 shows FID for C2000 at 20.43 (VE) and 19.06 (VP), far worse than others, so at equal step count (2000), it underperforms. But the parenthetical correctly notes that more steps are needed to close the gap. The issue arises in the framing: claiming 'same computation' while citing evidence that correctors are inefficient per step. The intended point—that correctors alone are inefficient—is valid, but the phrasing obscures the role of correctors as *complements* to predictors (their purpose is to correct predictor error, not to sample independently). Thus, the comparison is valid but poorly framed.

comments.title: Inconsistent model naming in Table 3 discussion
comments.quote: The resulting architectures are denoted as NCSN++ cont. and DDPM++ cont. in Table 3 for VE and VP/sub-VP SDEs respectively.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the naming convention appears inconsistent: 'NCSN++ cont.' and 'DDPM++ cont.' suggest distinct model families tied to specific SDEs (VE and VP/sub-VP), but the paper previously defines DDPM++ and NCSN++ as architectural improvements applicable across SDE frameworks. The concern is whether 'cont.' refers to continuous training (Eq. 7) or implies a structural distinction. Checking the context, 'cont.' likely stands for 'continuous' (as opposed to discrete training objectives), and the naming is used to distinguish continuously trained variants of the same architecture. However, the phrasing 'for VE and VP/sub-VP SDEs respectively' incorrectly implies a one-to-one mapping between NCSN++ and VE, and DDPM++ and VP/sub-VP, which is not accurate—both architectures can be applied to both SDEs. The paper later refers to 'DDPM++ cont. (deep, sub-VP)', confirming that architecture names are reused across SDE types. Thus, the phrasing misleads by suggesting architectural specialization where none exists. This is a minor framing issue resolvable from later usage.

comments.title: Contradiction between text and probability flow claim
comments.quote: FID scores and NLL values in Table 2 are reported for the last training checkpoint, and samples are obtained with black-box ODE solvers.
comments.explanation: This statement raises concern because the paper claims that the probability flow ODE enables exact likelihood computation (a key theoretical contribution), and Table 3 reports 'log-likelihood of 2.99 bits/dim'—presumably computed via the ODE. However, Table 2 reports NLL values obtained with ODE solvers on the 'last checkpoint', while Table 3 reports FID for the 'checkpoint with smallest FID'. If different checkpoints are used, comparing FID and NLL across tables conflates training dynamics with model performance. The issue is not the usage of ODE solvers per se, but the lack of alignment in evaluation strategy: one table uses best-FID checkpoint (favoring sample quality), another uses final checkpoint (favoring convergence). This undermines fair comparison between likelihood and sample quality across models. While common in practice, this introduces a methodological ambiguity about whether improvements are due to model design or checkpoint selection. The paper should clarify or use consistent checkpoint selection for fair benchmarking.

comments.title: Ambiguity in claim about VE vs VP sample quality and likelihood trade-off
comments.quote: As shown in Table 3, VE SDEs typically provide better sample quality than VP/sub-VP SDEs, but we also empirically observe that their likelihoods are worse than VP/sub-VP SDE counterparts.
comments.explanation: This claim attempts to establish a trade-off between sample quality (FID) and likelihood (NLL), but lacks sufficient support. The paper does not present NLL values for VE models in Table 3; only FID is reported. The claim about likelihoods being 'worse' for VE models is not backed by data in the provided context, though it may be in Table 2 (not quoted here). Without seeing the actual NLL comparison, this assertion risks misleading: one might infer a general trade-off when the difference could stem from training setup, architecture mismatch, or evaluation method (e.g., ODE vs SDE sampling). Moreover, the PAPER SUMMARY notes that 'DDPM++ with ... sub-VP SDE achieves state-of-the-art NLL', implying the sub-VP likelihood superiority, but this should be explicitly supported by numbers. The concern is minor because a trade-off is plausible and context suggests it, but the presentation implies empirical evidence not fully visible.

comments.title: Overlooked OCR issue in diffusion coefficient notation
comments.quote: and _g_ p _t_ q[2] r _∇_ **x** log _pt_ p **x** q ` _∇_ **x** log _pt_ p **y** | **x** qsud _t_ ` _g_ p _t_ qd ¯ **w** _._
comments.explanation: The expression '_g_ p _t_ q[2] r...' likely results from OCR: '[2]' is probably superscript 2, meaning g(t)^2. In the FULL PAPER CONTEXT, g(t) is the diffusion coefficient, and the reverse-time SDE is consistently given as involving g(t)^2 ∇ log p_t(x); e.g., in the probability flow ODE: 'g(t)^2 ∇ log p_t'. Here, '_g_ p _t_ q[2]' is intended to be g(t)^2. The rest of the equation matches standard reverse-time SDE form. Therefore, '[2]' should be interpreted as squared, and this is not a mathematical error but an artifact of text extraction. No correction needed under the assumption of plausible symbol recovery.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a remarkably clear and ambitious framework for score-based generative modeling, and the quality of its exposition in the abstract and introduction is exemplary. The authors masterfully situate their work within the context of existing SMLD and DDPM approaches, articulating a compelling and unified theoretical vision through the lens of stochastic differential equations (SDEs). The proposed unification is theoretically elegant, and the claimed capabilities—including flexible sampling via predictor-corrector and ODE methods, exact likelihood computation, and controllable generation from an unconditional model—are highly significant and represent substantial advances for the field. The most significant issue raised by this ambitious scope is the potential for a lack of focus, as the paper simultaneously introduces a major theoretical framework, two novel sampling methods, a new approach to inverse problems, and reports multiple state-of-the-art results. A critical evaluation will therefore depend on whether the full paper can substantiate each of these extensive claims with corresponding depth in both theoretical justification and experimental validation, or if the breadth of contributions comes at the expense of a thorough treatment of each individual component.

comments.title: OCR error in DDPM reverse process parameterization
comments.quote: A variational Markov chain 1 in the reverse direction is parameterized with _p_ _**θ**_ p **x** _i_ ´1| **x** _i_ q " _N_ p **x** _i_ ´1; ~~?~~ 1´ _βi_[p] **[x]** _[i]_[`] _[β][i]_ **[s]** _**[θ]**_[p] **[x]** _[i][, i]_[qq] _[, β][i]_ **[I]**[q][,]
comments.explanation: There's clearly an OCR error with the symbol '~~?~~' in the mean of the Gaussian distribution. Based on the context and standard DDPM notation, this should be 'μ_θ(x_i, i)'. The authors are parameterizing the reverse process mean as a function of the current state x_i and timestep i. This error makes the equation unreadable and obscures an important technical detail.

comments.title: Confusing Langevin MCMC notation
comments.quote: where _ϵi_ ą 0 is the step size, and **z** _[m] i_[is standard normal.]
comments.explanation: The notation **z**_i^{[m]} is used in the Langevin update equation before being defined. While it's clarified immediately after that it's standard normal, better practice would be to define it before use in the equation. The update equation appears to be missing from the provided context, but based on standard Langevin dynamics, it should be x_i^{[m+1]} = x_i^{[m]} + ε_i * s_θ(x_i^{[m]}, σ_i) + √(2ε_i) * z_i^{[m]}.

comments.title: Potentially confusing dt notation in reverse-time SDE
comments.quote: where **w** ¯ is a standard Wiener process when time flows backwards from _T_ to 0, and d _t_ is an infinitesimal negative timestep.
comments.explanation: The description of dt as 'an infinitesimal negative timestep' could be confusing to readers. In reverse-time SDEs, we integrate backward in time from T to 0, so dt is indeed negative. However, this phrasing might obscure the fact that the reverse-time SDE has the same mathematical form as a forward SDE but with different coefficients. It would be clearer to state that we integrate backward in time, making dt negative.

comments.title: Misleading proportional symbol in score matching
comments.quote: As in SMLD and DDPM, we can typically choose _λ_ 91{E"  _∇_ **x** p _t_ q log _p_ 0 _t_ p **x** p _t_ q | **x** p0qq2 ‰.
comments.explanation: The notation 'λ(t) ∝ 1/𝔼[...]' is misleading here. The text appears to be saying λ(t) equals the reciprocal of the expected squared score norm, not just proportional to it. The proportional symbol (∝) suggests there could be additional scaling factors, but the context implies equality. This could confuse readers about the exact form of the weighting function.

comments.title: Confusing notation in SMLD sampling initialization
comments.explanation: The notation for initializing the sampling chain is confusing. It says to initialize x_i^{[0]} = x_{i+1}^{[M]} when i < N, but since we're iterating backward (from N to 1), this creates confusion. It should be clearer that for i = N-1, ..., 1, we initialize with the final sample from the next noise level (i+1). The notation x_{i+1}^{[M]} is correct but the explanation could be clearer about the backward iteration.

comments.title: OCR error in Table 1 header
comments.quote: FIDÓ
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the header 'FIDÓ' appears to be an OCR error for 'FID' (Fréchet Inception Distance). The 'Ó' character is likely a misrecognition artifact. This is a clear OCR issue that doesn't affect the interpretation of the table since the metric being reported is clearly FID based on the values shown (which are in the typical range for CIFAR-10 FID scores from 2021). The authors use 'FID' elsewhere in the text consistently.

comments.title: Typo in Table 1: 'probabilityfow'
comments.quote: probabilityfow
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the row label 'probabilityfow' is missing the letter 'l' and should be 'probability flow'. This refers to the deterministic sampling method based on the probability flow ODE discussed in Section 4.3 (mentioned in the paper summary). This is a minor typo that doesn't affect understanding since the correct term is used elsewhere in the paper and context makes it clear.

comments.title: Unusually high FID scores for probability flow with VE SDE
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 1, the probability flow ODE performs poorly for the Variance Exploding (VE) SDE with FID scores of 15.41 (P1000) and 10.54 (P2000), which are much higher than ancestral sampling and reverse diffusion for the same SDE (around 4.8-5.0). However, for the Variance Preserving (VP) SDE, probability flow performs competitively (3.59 for P1000, 3.23 for P2000). This large discrepancy is notable because the paper claims the probability flow ODE enables 'improved sampling efficiency.' While this could be a genuine result (probability flow might work well for VP but not VE SDEs), the authors don't explain this discrepancy in the passage. The high FID scores for VE SDE with probability flow seem inconsistent with the general claim of improved efficiency, though they may be addressed elsewhere in the paper.

comments.title: PC1000 vs P2000 comparison for VP SDE
comments.quote: Moreover, it is typically better than doubling the number of predictor steps without adding a corrector (P2000), where we have to interpolate between noise scales in an ad hoc manner (detailed in Appendix G) for SMLD/DDPM models.
comments.explanation: The text states that PC1000 is 'typically better' than P2000, but looking at Table 1 for VP SDE (DDPM) models, the first row shows PC1000 has FID of 3.21 while P2000 has FID of 3.19. This means PC1000 is actually worse than P2000 for this case (lower FID is better). While the text does say 'typically better' which allows for exceptions, and the difference is small (0.02), this contradicts the claim being made. For VE SDE (SMLD), PC1000 (3.62) is indeed better than P2000 (4.88), so the claim holds there. The inconsistency is specific to the VP SDE case.

comments.title: Potentially misleading comparison to conditional models
comments.quote: Surprisingly, we can achieve better FID than the previous best conditional generative model without requiring labeled data.
comments.explanation: This claim compares the FID of their unconditional model to conditional models from prior work. However, FID is typically used to evaluate unconditional generation quality, and conditional models are usually evaluated on conditional tasks. Comparing unconditional FID scores to conditional models' FID scores (which may have been computed differently or in different contexts) could be misleading. The authors should clarify that they're not claiming their unconditional model outperforms conditional models on conditional generation tasks, but rather that their unconditional model's sample quality (as measured by FID) exceeds what was previously achieved by conditional models on unconditional generation.

comments.title: Underspecified classifier training method
comments.quote: Afterwards, we may employ a mixture of cross-entropy losses over different time steps, like Eq. (7), to train the time-dependent classifier pₜ(y|x(t)).
comments.explanation: The description of how to train the time-dependent classifier is vague. While referencing Eq. (7) provides some context, the exact formulation of the 'mixture of cross-entropy losses' is unclear. Key details missing include: how the losses from different time steps are weighted, whether the same time sampling strategy as Eq. (7) is used, and how this differs from standard classifier training. This makes it difficult for readers to reproduce the method.

comments.title: Overstated claim about solving inverse problems
comments.quote: In general, we can use Eq. (14) to solve a large family of inverse problems with score-based generative models, once given an estimate of the gradient of the forward process, ∇ₓlog pₜ(y|x(t)).
comments.explanation: This is a strong claim that Eq. (14) can solve 'a large family of inverse problems.' While the framework is theoretically general, practical application requires being able to compute ∇ₓlog pₜ(y|x(t)), which may be intractable or require problem-specific approximations for many inverse problems. The authors later mention they provide a 'broadly applicable method' in Appendix I.4, but without seeing this appendix, the claim seems overstated. The statement should be qualified to acknowledge the practical challenges in computing the required gradients.

comments.explanation: The description of how to train the time-dependent classifier is vague. While referencing Eq. (7) provides some context, the exact formulation of the 'mixture of cross-entropy losses' is unclear. Key details missing include: how the losses from different time steps are weighted, whether the same time sampling strategy as Eq. (7) is used, and how this differs from standard classifier training. This makes it difficult for readers to reproduce the method.

comments.title: Overstated claim about solving inverse problems
comments.quote: In general, we can use Eq. (14) to solve a large family of inverse problems with score-based generative models, once given an estimate of the gradient of the forward process, ∇ₓlog pₜ(y|x(t)).
comments.explanation: This is a strong claim that Eq. (14) can solve 'a large family of inverse problems.' While the framework is theoretically general, practical application requires being able to compute ∇ₓlog pₜ(y|x(t)), which may be intractable or require problem-specific approximations for many inverse problems. The authors later mention they provide a 'broadly applicable method' in Appendix I.4, but without seeing this appendix, the claim seems overstated. The statement should be qualified to acknowledge the practical challenges in computing the required gradients.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a rigorous and highly influential theoretical unification of existing generative modeling paradigms by framing the denoising process as a continuous-time stochastic differential equation (SDE). The writing is exceptionally clear, effectively motivating the shift from discrete noise corruption to a smooth forward and reverse process, and the introduction cleanly structures the presentation of novel contributions, including exact likelihood computation and a predictor-corrector framework. By integrating SMLD and DDPM under one theoretical roof, the authors resolve several prior discrepancies and lay the groundwork for advanced capabilities like controlled generation using a single, pretrained model. The most significant potential issue at this stage lies in the transition from the high-level conceptual overview to the formal mathematical definitions; the rigorous proof of the derived reverse-time SDEs and the implementation details provided in the background sections will be critical to ensuring the proposed framework is mathematically sound and reproducible for the broader community.

comments.title: Missing explicit SDE definitions
comments.quote: An overview of our framework is given in Fig. 2. (5) 3 Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021 where **w** is the standard Wiener process...
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the passage introduces equation (5) as the definition of the forward SDE but immediately cuts to a page number '3 Published...' without rendering the mathematical formula `dx_t = f(x_t, t) dt + g(t) dw_t`. Similarly, in Section 3.2, the passage lists 'reverse-time SDE:' but the actual formula for the backward process is missing from the text block, appearing only as the label before the description of coefficients. While the text describes the coefficients (f, g, w) afterwards, the core definition of the SDE is not accessible in the provided passage.

comments.title: Notation ambiguity in DDPM reverse kernel
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, the notation for the reverse Markov transition kernel uses `x_i - 1` (with a space) instead of `x_{i-1}` (with a subscript). In a mathematical context involving sequences or time steps, this introduces a potential confusion with subtraction ($x_i - 1$) and obscures the functional relationship required by the Markov chain.

comments.title: OCR-corrupted equation formula
comments.explanation: The formula defining the reverse variational distribution in DDPM (Eq. 3) is almost entirely obscured by OCR artifacts. The intended structure of the Gaussian mean term (containing terms like sqrt(1-beta) and the network prediction epsilon) is unreadable, making it impossible to verify the mathematical consistency of the equation in this passage.

comments.title: Ambiguous transition density notation
comments.explanation: Section 3.3 uses the notation `p_0_t` to denote the transition density from the initial time `0` to time `t`. While the context (being a conditional distribution given `x_0`) clarifies its meaning, the standard notation in stochastic processes is usually `p_t(x_t | x_0)` or `p(x_t | x_0)`. The notation `p_0_t` is non-standard and could be a minor source of confusion for a reader encountering it for the first time.

comments.title: Incorrect Table Citation
comments.quote: As an example, we report negative log-likelihoods (NLLs) measured in bits/dim on the CIFAR-10 dataset in Table 2.
comments.explanation: The passage introduces a list of model results and their corresponding NLLs/FIDs to illustrate the claims about exact likelihood computation. While the text refers to this data as being in 'Table 2', the list of model entries (NCSN++, DDPM++, DDPM cont., etc.) clearly corresponds to 'Table 3: CIFAR-10 sample quality' in the original paper context (and as labeled in the OCR snippet 'Table 3'). The cross-reference error implies the reader would likely look in the wrong table section for this specific data.

comments.title: Notation Mismatch in Text and Table
comments.quote: Using the same architecture, we trained another DDPM model with the continuous objective in Eq. (7) ( i.e ., DDPM cont...)... For the same DDPM model in Ho et al. (2020), we obtain better bits/dim than ELBO [i.e., the row labeled 'DDPM (_L_)']
comments.explanation: In the provided passage, the text consistently refers to a model trained with the continuous objective as 'DDPM cont'. However, in the table (Table 3), this specific model is simply labeled 'DDPM', while a more specific variant 'DDPM cont. (VP)' exists. This slight naming inconsistency between the prose and the tabular data could confuse a reader attempting to map the specific description to the corresponding numerical results.

comments.title: Overstated Comparative Claim
comments.quote: We observe that our reverse diffusion sampler always outperform ancestral sampling
comments.explanation: Table 1 lists results for the VP SDE. For the 'Predictor Sampler' (P1000), the FID for 'reverse diffusion' is 3.24, while 'ancestral sampling' is also 3.24. While the reverse diffusion sampler outperforms ancestral sampling in other cases (e.g., P2000 vs Ancestral), the use of the word 'always' makes the claim technically inaccurate for the specific P1000 VP case shown in the table.

comments.title: Ambiguous Method Description
comments.quote: manipulate this latent representation for image editing, such as interpolation, and temperature scaling
comments.explanation: The text mentions 'temperature scaling' as a specific manipulation for the latent representation derived from the probability flow ODE. While multiplying the score function by a scalar (temperature) is a valid technique in score-based generative modeling, it is less standard terminology for ODE-based latent spaces than 'interpolation'. The term 'temperature scaling' typically refers to GANs or classification thresholds. It is not a technical error, but the phrasing is slightly unusual for this context.
